mnemonics.title: Normal Forms Order
mnemonics.content:
1NF: Atomic values (Single values)
2NF: No Partial (Full dependency)
3NF: No Transitive (No chain)
BCNF: Superkey must be (All keys) - Remember: APT (Atomic, Partial, Transitive, Boyce)

mnemonics.title: Normal Form Purposes
mnemonics.content:
1NF: Make values single and atomic
2NF: Remove partial dependencies from composite keys
3NF: Remove transitive dependencies (non-key to non-key)
BCNF: Make all determinants superkeys

mnemonics.title: Anomaly Types
mnemonics.content:
Insert- Cannot add without extra
Update- Same data in many places
Delete- Remove one, lose another
Remember: I (nsert), U(pdate), D(elete) = IUD problems

acronyms.usage: Ensures atomic values in each column
Eliminates partial dependencies for composite keys
Stricter version of 3NF, all determinants are superkeys
Minimal superkey, can be primary key
Attribute part of any candidate key
Attribute not part of any candidate key

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with repeated groups vs atomic table. Show: {Phone1, Phone2} in one cell is WRONG, Phone1 in one row, Phone2 in another row is 1NF correct format.
For composite key (A,B)→C, show arrow from A alone pointing to C (partial dependency - BAD), and arrow from (A,B) pointing to C (full dependency - GOOD).
Show chain: A→B (key to non-key), then B→C (non-key to non-key). This transitive chain is BAD in 3NF. Break it by splitting tables.
Use Venn diagram: BCNF circle inside 3NF circle inside 2NF circle. Show that BCNF is stricter - more decompositions needed.
Draw arrow X→Y: X on left (determinant) with key symbol, Y on right (dependent). Show one-to-one or one-to-many relationship visually.

analogies.analogy: Think of a messy desk with papers everywhere (redundant data). Normalization is like organizing into labeled folders and drawers - everything in its proper place, no duplicates.
Like a spice rack - each jar contains ONE spice, not a mix. You don't have a jar labeled 'salt-pepper-sugar' - that would be non-atomic.
Like a recipe book. If you have 'Breakfast Recipe' and 'Egg Recipe', having 'Egg' listed under both is like partial dependency. In 2NF, recipes should reference ingredients, not duplicate them.
Like address format: City→ZIP Code→State. If you store State directly from ZIP in one table (transitive), that's problematic. Better to have separate tables linked by keys.
Like a lock and key - if you have the key (X), you can open the lock (Y). The key determines what's behind the lock.

Think of a shared Google Doc where multiple people edit. Insert anomaly: can't add new person without creating document. Update anomaly: changing name in one place doesn't update others. Delete anomaly: removing one person deletes shared info.
